lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: I have two nights watched with you, but can perceive no truth

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: in your report. When was it she last walked?

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Since his majesty went into the field, I have seen her rise

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: from her bed, throw her nightgown upon her, unlock her closet, take

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: forth paper, fold it, write upon’t, read it, afterwards seal it, and

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: again return to bed; yet all this while in a most fast

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: sleep.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: A great perturbation in nature, to receive at once the benefit of

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: sleep, and do the effects of watching! In this slumbery agitation, besides

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: her walking and other actual performances, what, at any time, have you

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: heard her say?

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: That, sir, which I will not report after her.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: You may to me: and ’tis most meet you should.

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Neither to you nor any one; having no witness to confirm my speech.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: How came she by that light?

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Why, it stood by her: she has light by her continually; ’tis her command.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: You see, her eyes are open.

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Ay, but their sense is shut.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: What is it she does now? Look, how she rubs her hands.

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: It is an accustomed action with her, to seem thus washing her

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: hands: I have known her continue in this a quarter of an

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: hour.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Yet here’s a spot.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Hark! she speaks: I will set down what comes from her, to

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: satisfy my remembrance the more strongly.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Out, damned spot! out, I say!—One: two: why, then, ’tis time to

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: do’t.—Hell is murky!—Fie, my lord, fie! a soldier, and afeard? What need

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: we fear who knows it, when none can call our power to

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: account?—Yet who would have thought the old man to have had so

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: much blood in him.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Do you mark that?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: The thane of Fife had a wife: where is she now?—What, will

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: these hands ne’er be clean?—No more o’ that, my lord, no more

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: o’ that: you mar all with this starting.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Go to, go to; you have known what you should not.

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: She has spoke what she should not, I am sure of that:

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: heaven knows what she has known.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Here’s the smell of the blood still: all the perfumes of Arabia

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: will not sweeten this little hand. Oh, oh, oh!

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: What a sigh is there! The heart is sorely charged.

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: I would not have such a heart in my bosom for the

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: dignity of the whole body.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Well, well, well—

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Pray God it be, sir.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: This disease is beyond my practice: yet I have known those which

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: have walked in their sleep who have died holily in their beds.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Wash your hands, put on your nightgown; look not so pale.—I tell

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: you yet again, Banquo’s buried; he cannot come out on’s grave.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Even so?

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: To bed, to bed! there’s knocking at the gate: come, come, come,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: come, give me your hand. What’s done cannot be undone.—To bed, to

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: bed, to bed!

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Will she go now to bed?

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Directly.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Foul whisperings are abroad: unnatural deeds

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Do breed unnatural troubles: infected minds

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: To their deaf pillows will discharge their secrets:

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: More needs she the divine than the physician.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: God, God forgive us all! Look after her;

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Remove from her the means of all annoyance,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: And still keep eyes upon her. So, good night:

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: My mind she has mated, and amazed my sight.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: I think, but dare not speak.

lines.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
lines.text: Good night, good doctor.

stage_directions.text: Enter a Doctor of Physic and a Waiting-Gentlewoman.
Enter Lady Macbeth, with a taper.
Lo you, here she comes! This is her very guise; and, upon my life, fast asleep. Observe her; stand close.

glosses.definition: Lady Macbeth's reversal — the same woman who mocked Macbeth's fear of hell now sees into it
stop — the subjects she cannot bear to remember

glosses.phrase: Out, damned spot! out, I say!
glosses.definition: the sleepwalking scene's central line; the stain she dismissed in 2.2 cannot be washed out — the water cannot clear her of the deed
the bloodstain she cannot remove; the moral mark now physically visible to her alone
for shame, my lord — her voice from 2.2
Arabia's famed spices cannot sweeten her little hand — the finest luxuries of the Renaissance world inadequate to the stain of regicide
the nobility of her whole person
a priest — not a physician, but a confessor, is what she needs

annotations.title: The observers and the form of confession
annotations.body:
Shakespeare frames the sleepwalking scene with two silent witnesses: the Doctor of Physic and the Gentlewoman. Neither may repeat what Lady Macbeth says, but both are forced to hear. The form is the confession — sacramentally, the priest must not repeat what he hears. Shakespeare has placed a priest-stand-in at a scene of unconscious confession by a woman who cannot stop speaking.

The structural choice matters. We have not heard Lady Macbeth's inner voice since 3.2. Between then and now, the play gave her silence. What she stopped saying — about the murder, about her husband, about her conscience — pooled and returned in her sleep. The two witnesses allow the audience to hear what Lady Macbeth could not say while awake. The prohibition on repetition is Shakespeare's acknowledgment that what we are hearing should not be heard at all.

annotations.title: The habitual action of washing
annotations.body:
'Accustomed action with her, to seem thus washing her hands. I have known her continue in this a quarter of an hour.' Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking has ritualized itself. The hand-washing of 2.2 — 'A little water clears us of this deed' — has become a permanent, nightly, fifteen-minute automation.

The specific minute-count is the scene's most chilling detail. We are not watching a one-off outburst; we are watching a private liturgy that has been repeating for months. The water Lady Macbeth pours is the water that, by 2.2, she said would be sufficient; it has been proving itself insufficient nightly for the entire third act.

The play's most important revision of her confidence comes at line 55: 'all the perfumes of Arabia will not sweeten this little hand.' The Arabian spice trade was the Renaissance emblem of the most expensive commodity in the world. If the Arabian perfume cannot reach it, nothing can. Lady Macbeth is making, in her sleep, the argument Macbeth made to her awake in 2.2 — the 'multitudinous seas' will rather take on the blood than wash it off.

annotations.title: Her spiritual diagnosis
annotations.body:
The Doctor's final line — 'More needs she the divine than the physician' — names what has happened to Lady Macbeth. Her illness is not medical but spiritual. The 'unnatural deeds' of 1.5 and 2.2 have produced 'unnatural troubles': sleep without rest, sight without vision, speech without listener. These are the classical symptoms of a soul separated from grace.

The play does not let Lady Macbeth die on stage. Her death is reported in 5.5, offstage, and Macbeth barely responds. Shakespeare's choice is precise: Lady Macbeth's death is not a scene but a rumor. She has become unrepresentable — not because she lacks dramatic weight, but because the scene we have just watched is her death. Everything after it is postscript.

trials.prompt: Why are the Doctor and Gentlewoman both present?
options: They function as priest-stand-ins at an unconscious confession — forced to hear, forbidden to repeat

trials.prompt: The Gentlewoman notes Lady Macbeth continues the hand-washing 'a quarter of an hour.' This reveals:
options: A nightly ritual that has been repeating for months — a private liturgy

trials.prompt: 'All the perfumes of Arabia will not sweeten this little hand' functions as:
options: Lady Macbeth making, in her sleep, the argument Macbeth made to her awake in 2.2 — blood cannot be washed off

trials.prompt: The Doctor concludes 'More needs she the divine than the physician' because:
options: Her illness is spiritual, not medical — the symptoms of a soul separated from grace

trials.prompt: Lady Macbeth mentions three murders in her sleep. They are:
options: Duncan, Banquo, Macduff's family (the Thane of Fife's wife)
